speaker: interviewer
text: --Seven survivors, Abraham Bomba. The date is August 14, 1996. Mr Bomba, what are you presently doing right now? Are you retired?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you do in your spare time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, in my spare time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some think, and I go into club, play, swim. But I do also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] go into schools organized by the Holocaust survivors of South Florida, and-- to talk to the children in the school about the Holocaust, what I went through, like other people, they talking what they went through. And that is a big satisfaction for me, to speak in front of those children. And the question what those children are asking, which had happened many, many time I spoke to grown up people. But they didn't had the nerve or they didn't have their mind to ask question like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the children ask you, like what? Give me an example.

speaker: interviewee
text: The same thing. I would say, first of all, how come that you survived and so many or your people did not? And I want you to mention that not children, Jewish children-- that the children from Catholic school, public school, high school. And I don't think that I, I had one of those students were Jewish, which I didn't ask. But you cannot ask in a school who is Jewish, who is not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But very, very interesting question, what had happened to the people, what did happen to their mind? What happened to the children? How they acted, and how could the German do something like that what they did? They didn't had anything in their heart or in their mind what they doing. And that was very interesting for me, those question and to explain that to them-- the way they were, and what they did. Mr. Bomba, do you have any thoughts, theories, speculations? Why did you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know, I believe that is a way-- if I would be very religion, I would say that's God's way that I survived. What a way it is. I don't know why I survived. And I don't know, going through so many times being just on a step from being dead until now. And especially with my sickness lately, that I survive, I don't know. It's something must keep-- somebody, an eye on me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever dream about any of your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The begin-- the beginning, the beginning many times, which night couldn't sleep. To think is, the dreaming about is they're running after me, and they wanted to take me into the gas chamber. And I escaped, and I escaped. And I dream again. And when you get up and fall asleep, the dream comes back. It is lately not as much as from the beginning. It is a horrible thing to live at day, and at night to live through the story where you went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: What advice would you like to give to your daughter, your grandchildren, the world? After having survived what you did, after being in Treblinka, what would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There is not any advice what I could give. My advice only to people, to my children is there should be human being, should be nice to all the people. It doesn't matter what nation. It doesn't matter what color they are. And there should be-- in Jewish, we say mensch. That means it brings around that everything. Because if somebody is really a human being, he will never do anything wrong to another human being.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to say at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think I said already enough. Oh, you want to have this thing here. But I will not sound-- even with a my translation or any other translation, it's not the same as a poem is said in the original thing here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is-- can you tell me about this poem? What are you going to read now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now this is a poem about taking-- how would we say in English? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH]? How could you say?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you write this poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I got two of them. That is-- I would say, take advantage [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of the, of the thing what you went through, where you were sent. And there will come-- there will come a time when you take not exactly adventure. But you will fight against them. You will hate them. And you will live the time when you will be liberated. It's a very-- it's-- I would say it is very nice. I got two of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wrote this yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your original poem?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in what language are you going to read it first?

speaker: interviewee
text: The old Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're going to read it in Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish. That's all in Yiddish. In, in, in English I don't write. In anything else I didn't write, only those two.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you will read the first poem now in Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now that, the poem goes like that. It called [YIDDISH]. It says, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't understand nothing from it. Is that what you translated before?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What you said before, is that what that meant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Say, [SPEAKING YIDDISH] Now the story goes like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People, they don't know what happened. But it's time to tell them and there will-- there will be a time to take revenge on the Germans. It's also in people that were in all those concentration camps. No other people know what had happened. But just ask those people, and they will tell you. Now it also, it happened that somebody wants to run away from the ghetto, from the concentration camp. He has money, he goes to the barbed wire. And he tries to buy off thus. But this guy, he takes the money. He kills the people. Now there is also a thing that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the war goes on. But United State is not in a hurry. England, the same thing. The Russian-- they going, but they are far away yet. But in the meantime, another action brought out. They took out from the factory 5,000 people, and they killed them. That is plus minus the meaning thereof. Now this is one, the other one is-- in another time. Now this one is about the time when it broke out the ghetto. The, the action against the Jewish people. That's the minute when they took out the people. It says [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just briefly summarize what that means in English?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will finish this. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] oh, three, seven, eight, 10. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Now this is the story exactly what had happened. It happened on the day of the Yom Kippur, when the action in Czestochowa started. They took the people out, and they sent to the transport. Some of them, like myself, were sent back. And the other one went away to Treblinka. It was in the city joy and horror and tears. Some of them got there with family. Some of them, they don't have it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now they were taking from other places. And also the same thing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It can-- I memorize it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they throw the people in, just like balls, in the train and, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and one was on top of the other one. And the one who were already inside, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what a lot of people in. And they didn't know what is going to happen to them. Some say we going to the-- to work. And some say we going in a fire, where they're going to kill us. But everyone keeps this bundle of everything what he has. When we came to the city, it was already away from the railroad station going through the city of our town, Czestochowa. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now we know where we're going. Because on the other side of the train was another train, with the gentiles. One gentile spit on us. And the other gentile cried out. He said, you're going to Treblinka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Bomba, I want to thank you very much for your testimony.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome, you are very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your interview.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] This is the picture of my mother. Her name was Touba Kozuch. Married name was Bomba. And the child she's holding is mine brother, mine oldest brother, by the name of Jacob. At that time, he was about six months old. The picture was taken about 83 years ago, in Czestochowa, Poland. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture is of myself. My name is Abraham Leib Bomba. The picture was taken in Czestochowa. The picture was taken in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 1913, which is almost 83 years old. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Oh, this picture was taken in Germany, in Falkenstein, in 1946 or '47. From the left side is Yechiel Kruzer. Next to him, cousin of mine, Hanka Backenrode. Out Next to her is my wife, Regina Bomba, and myself, Abraham Leib Bomba. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in Treblinka. Treblinka was destroyed after the uprising. And all what they have in Treblinka is big stones. Each stone has a name of a city or a country from where the people were brought, from their places to Treblinka, where they perished over there in the gas chambers. Next to that picture over there, next to the stone is myself, Abraham Leib Bomba.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- when did you go back there? What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back there four years ago. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh, this a picture of a Torah. The Torah is from Czestochowa, brought by this young man, Harry Rappaport. The Torah was buried 50 years in Czestochowa. Was finded by Harry Rappaport, brought illegal out from the communist country to America. Restored, which took already two years, and this is in my possession. It-- it has the name of my father and mother, my wife's father and mother, and all of the people, 6 million people who perished during the Holocaust. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in Israel, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about six years ago. That picture is a friend of mine, Sam Willenberg, a very well-known man. Wrote a book about Treblinka. He was in Treblinka from the beginning till the end. He took part in the uprising. Next to him is my wife. And to the right side is his wife. Very, very well-known man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- as you're looking at the picture, who is on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left is his wife, Sam Willenberg's wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the right is your wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is my wife, yes. Now this is a picture, a very close friend of mine. He was in Treblinka. He worked at the second camp, next to the gas chamber. He was there from the beginning until the uprising in Treblinka. His name is Pinchus Epstein. His wife's name is Golda. He was the main witness at Demjanjuk trial in Israel. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Oh, this is a picture when I was about four years ago in Treblinka. It-- I was taking outside Treblinka, which shows the road to Treblinka. That's myself and a friend of mine who lives in Detroit. His name-- I think, I'm not too sure-- is Tarosky or Tarnosky. I don't remember exactly how it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are you in this picture? Are you on the left, in the blue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm, I'm at the left, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Bomba, would you introduce your wife, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this is my lovely wife, Regina Bomba. Her maiden's name is Hamburger. She is the daughter of Oizer Bezalel and Bela Hamburger. We both survivors. My wife was in the ghetto, in a concentration camp. And thank god we are together now over 50 years. I'm sorry our family cannot be here. My daughter, with a small child, is in Florida. And my grandchildren, they are in Israel. Myself, my name is Abraham Bomba. I am a survivor of Treblinka and of concentration camp [? Hasak. ?] And I would like to thank all those people which they are interested to show the world what Shoah Holocaust meant to us. And let the people not forget what had happened and should never happen again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Bomba, would you like to say something to your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we met during the war, in the camp. I went to ask him if he didn't see my parents or mine first husband. And he told me what happened over there. I couldn't believe it, what he told me, that they're killing people and gassing people over there. And then he came to my house. And we start going out. And he fell in love with me, and I fell in love with him. And we waited for a couple of years, until the war ended. And after the war, we got married. And we have one daughter. She lived 18 years in Israel. Now she lives in the United States, [INAUDIBLE] with us, in Florida. Again, my daughter is with us now. Tomorrow she's leaving for Israel. Tomorrow, 2 o'clock she's leaving. And I called her, I called and I told I'm coming in 50 minutes. She doesn't know what to think anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about my husband? He's a very good husband, a very good man. I love him a lot. And let us live another couple of years, I hope. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're very welcome.
